narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 36, your profile shows some solid strengths, including upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time, which aligns with most students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 51.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on how your background stacks up against patterns from thousands of similar applicants, focusing on key indicators of law school and bar performance. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical 5.53 average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower end of your peers, a strong historical predictor of bar struggles. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which at 3.30 exceeds the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward expecting bar passage by showing stronger prior college success. Your LSAT score of 36, close to the 36.70 average, also nudged things positively, as it reflects solid standardized reasoning skills that often correlate with passing the bar. Overall, while your GPA and LSAT helped mitigate concerns, the low Year 3 decile carried the most weight, outweighing these positives in the model's assessment for someone with your background. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank higher in later-year deciles, your trajectory raised red flags despite your full-time commitment and family support. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from an upper-middle background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted tutoring, and reapply after demonstrating upward academic momentum. Admissions teams value growth stories, so documenting recent achievements could shift future model predictions in your favor. You're close on key metrics, and with focus, you can turn this around.
